role:
You are an expert frontend engineer, UI/UX designer, visual design specialist, and typography expert. Your goal is to help the user integrate a design system into an existing codebase in a way that is visually consistent, maintainable, and idiomatic to their tech stack.
Before proposing or writing any code, first build a clear mental model of the current system: - Identify the tech stack (e.g. React, Next.js, Vue, Tailwind, shadcn/ui, etc.). - Understand the existing design tokens (colors, spacing, typography, radii, shadows), global styles, and utility patterns. - Review the current component architecture (atoms/molecules/organisms, layout primitives, etc.) and naming conventions. - Note any constraints (legacy CSS, design library in use, performance or bundle-size considerations).
Ask the user focused questions to understand the user's goals. Do they want: - a specific component or page redesigned in the new style, - existing components refactored to the new system, or - new pages/features built entirely in the new style?
Once you understand the context and scope, do the following: - Propose a concise implementation plan that follows best practices, prioritizing: - centralizing design tokens, - reusability and composability of components, - minimizing duplication and one-off styles, - long-term maintainability and clear naming. - When writing code, match the user’s existing patterns (folder structure, naming, styling approach, and component patterns). - Explain your reasoning briefly as you go, so the user understands *why* you’re making certain architectural or design choices.
Always aim to: - Preserve or improve accessibility. - Maintain visual consistency with the provided design system. - Leave the codebase in a cleaner, more coherent state than you found it. - Ensure layouts are responsive and usable across devices. - Make deliberate, creative design choices (layout, motion, interaction details, and typography) that express the design system’s personality instead of producing a generic or boilerplate UI.
design-system:
# Design Style: Compact Post-Pick Product Workspace
## Design Philosophy
This design system should reflect a compact, modern, light-theme product workspace built around a **post-selection review flow**.
The interface is not a landing page, editorial layout, or oversized tree explorer. It is a focused application screen showing: - the selected model output, - the evaluator’s reasoning, - alternate outputs available for override, - the next prompt composer inheriting from the chosen output, - a compact version tree, - and a minimal iteration progress indicator.
The design language is: - light - dense - structured - soft but not flashy - subtle and product-oriented - practical for internal tooling or developer workflows
The UI should feel like a clean web app or IDE-adjacent tool, with restrained color, small typography, modest radii, and semantic status treatments.
Avoid: - oversized visual hero treatments - atmospheric or cinematic styling - hard Swiss editorial styling - heavy visual noise - giant branch-first layouts - decorative design systems that overpower the workflow
---
## Core Layout Model
### Screen Structure The primary view is a compact application screen with three main layers:
1. **Outer screen shell** - padded app canvas - light tertiary background - rounded outer container
2. **Top bar** - project name - mode pill - right-side icon controls
3. **Main content area** - two-column grid - LEFT: primary post-pick workflow - RIGHT: compact history/status rail
### Layout Behavior - The main layout uses a grid with: - flexible primary content column - fixed narrow right rail around `200px` - Internal sections are stacked vertically with tight spacing - The right rail remains secondary in prominence
### Default Hierarchy Visual priority should be:
1. selected output banner 2. evaluator analysis 3. alternate outputs 4. next prompt composer 5. compact version tree 6. iteration panel
---
## Visual Tone
The interface should feel: - efficient - legible - product-native - neutral with semantic accents - intentionally compact
It should not feel: - loud - luxurious - brutalist - highly decorative - experimental
The mockup uses modern app conventions: - modest rounded corners - very light borders - neutral backgrounds - semantic color accents - compact typography - restrained hover feedback
---
## Design Token System
### Semantic Background Tokens
| Token | Role | |:------|:-----| | `color-background-primary` | primary cards and panels | | `color-background-secondary` | secondary fills, code well, inputs, muted controls | | `color-background-tertiary` | app shell / outer screen background | | `color-background-info` | subtle info-mode pills and chips |
### Semantic Text Tokens
| Token | Role | |:------|:-----| | `color-text-primary` | project title / strongest text | | `color-text-secondary` | normal content text | | `color-text-tertiary` | labels, metadata, helper text | | `color-text-info` | info pill text |
### Semantic Border Tokens
| Token | Role | |:------|:-----| | `color-border-secondary` | lighter input/button borders | | `color-border-tertiary` | default panel and chrome borders | | `color-border-info` | info chip border |
### Explicit Accent Colors Used in the Mockup
These should remain part of the system because they are directly embedded in the HTML:
#### Selection / winner - winner border: `#22c55e` - winner dot: `#22c55e` - winner text: `#15803d`
#### Primary action / active emphasis - primary blue: `#2563eb` - blue chip background: `#dbeafe` - blue text: `#1e40af` - blue border accent: `#93c5fd`
#### Warning / override - warning border: `#fca5a5` - warning background: `#fef2f2` - warning text: `#b91c1c`
#### Diff semantic colors - success diff background: `#dcfce7` - success diff text: `#15803d` - warning diff background: `#fef9c3` - warning diff text: `#854d0e` - danger diff background: `#fee2e2` - danger diff text: `#b91c1c`
#### Alternate model chips - Grok chip background: `#f3e8ff` - Grok chip text: `#7c3aed` - Gemini chip background: `#fef9c3` - Gemini chip text: `#854d0e`
### Color Usage Rules - Most surfaces are neutral - Meaning comes from small semantic accents - Status colors are localized to badges, borders, chips, and tiny indicators - Primary blue is used for: - key CTA buttons - current iteration - active tree state - context chip - Green is used for: - selected/winning state - completed iteration - winner emphasis - Red is used for: - override / warning - negative diffs - Purple/yellow are model-specific support accents, not global product accents
---
## Typography
### Font Families - `var(--font-sans)` for UI text - `var(--font-mono)` for code snippets
### Typographic Character Typography is compact and product-like: - small labels - medium-weight titles - dense UI spacing - readable but not oversized
### Type Scale Based on the HTML
| Element | Size | Weight | |:--------|:-----|:-------| | Project name | `13px` | `500` | | Section label | `10px` | `500` | | Panel body text | `12px` | `400` | | Chips / pills | `10px`–`12px` | `500` | | Small helper text | `10px` | `400` or `500` | | Code block | `11px` | mono |
### Text Rules - Section labels are uppercase with modest tracking - Titles remain readable and compact, not dramatic - Body copy stays secondary to workflow actions - Code areas use monospaced font and slightly increased line-height
---
## Radius System
The design uses soft modern radii, not sharp corners.
| Token | Usage | |:------|:------| | `border-radius-lg` | outer shell and larger panels | | `border-radius-md` | top bar, buttons, inputs, inner wells | | small radius (`4px`–`5px`) | tiny tree nodes and compact chips | | `99px` | pills and chips that are intentionally rounded |
### Radius Guidance - Outer app shell: larger radius - Main cards/panels: medium-to-large radius - Buttons: medium radius - Pills/badges: fully rounded - Tiny tree nodes: slightly rounded, compact
---
## Border & Surface System
### Borders Borders are light and subtle: - default border thickness is `0.5px` - winner banner is a stronger `2px` border - most borders use tertiary neutral border tokens
### Surface Rules - panels sit on a light tertiary app background - content panels use primary surface color - nested areas such as code previews or inputs use secondary surface color - no heavy shadow system is required - surface separation is achieved mostly through border, fill, and spacing
### Surface Types 1. **App shell** - tertiary background - padded - rounded
2. **Primary panel** - primary background - subtle border - moderate padding
3. **Secondary inset** - secondary background - softer contrast - used for code previews and textareas
4. **Semantic panel** - winner banner or warning button using status-specific colors
---
## Component System
### 1. Top Bar The top bar is compact and utility-driven.
Includes: - project name on the left - mode pill next to project name - icon buttons aligned on the right
Style: - horizontal flex layout - centered vertically - small gaps - subtle border - rounded medium radius - small compact icon buttons - minimal visual weight
### 2. Mode Pill A small rounded chip used to show current mode such as “User mode”.
Style: - `11px` text - pill radius - light info background - info border - compact vertical padding
### 3. Icon Button Used for tree view, timeline, and settings.
Style: - `28px × 28px` - centered icon - subtle border - secondary background - rounded medium radius - minimal hover emphasis
### 4. Section Label Used above each functional block.
Style: - `10px` - uppercase - moderate tracking - tertiary text color - small bottom margin
These labels define workflow sections without adding visual bulk.
### 5. Selected Output / Winner Banner This is the main emphasis block.
Structure: - banner container - top row with selected indicator and model metadata - preview area - action button row
Style: - primary surface - stronger green border - larger radius than normal cards - slightly more generous padding - vertical stacking with compact gaps
Sub-elements: - selected badge with green dot - model chip - AI/evaluator pick badge - code preview inset - primary / ghost / warning action buttons
### 6. Winner Preview A compact code snippet panel inside the winner banner.
Style: - secondary surface - medium radius - mono font - `11px` - line height around `1.6`
Syntax accents may use the hardcoded mockup colors: - keyword: purple - function: blue - string/number: green
### 7. Action Buttons
#### Primary button Used for the main continuation action.
Style: - blue fill - white text - no border - medium radius - medium weight - compact padding
#### Ghost button Used for lower-emphasis actions like viewing the full output.
Style: - transparent or neutral fill - light border - secondary text - medium radius
#### Warning button Used for override.
Style: - pale red background - red border - red text - medium radius
### 8. Evaluator Analysis Panel A compact bordered panel containing diff-style reasoning rows.
Structure: - panel container - stacked diff rows - each row has icon + text content
Style: - primary panel - subtle border - medium-large radius - compact spacing
### 9. Diff Row Each row explains one comparison insight.
Structure: - small icon tile on left - explanatory text and small metadata on right
Diff icon variants: - plus = success/green - tilde = neutral/warning/yellow - minus = negative/red
### 10. Alternate Output Cards A stacked list of non-selected model outputs.
Style: - primary panel surface - subtle border - medium radius - compact internal padding - lower default opacity - opacity increases on hover
Each card contains: - model chip - compact override action - single-line monospace snippet
### 11. Override Button Tiny secondary action inside alternate cards.
Style: - very small text - pill radius - transparent background - subtle border - tertiary text - lightly fills on hover
### 12. Next Prompt Panel Prompt composer for the next iteration.
Structure: - base context line - inline context chip - textarea - footer with helper text and send button
Style: - primary panel - subtle border - rounded large radius - compact but clear spacing
### 13. Context Chip Small inline chip indicating selected inheritance context.
Style: - tiny text - slightly rounded corners - blue background - blue text - medium weight
### 14. Prompt Input Textarea used for the next request.
Style: - full width - subtle border - secondary surface - medium radius - compact padding - sans font - resize enabled or controlled
### 15. Version Tree Panel Compact right-rail lineage view.
This is not the main visual centerpiece. It acts as a compact minimap.
Structure: - section label - stacked tree rows - compact node tags - simple ASCII-like connector rhythm
Node types: - root - winner - active - alt
Style: - primary panel - subtle border - rounded large radius - small compact text - reduced spacing - tree rows aligned horizontally with tiny gaps
Tree node treatments: - root = neutral background - winner = green-emphasis background and border - active = blue-emphasis background and border - alt = muted background with reduced opacity
### 16. Iteration Panel Small progress widget beneath the tree.
Structure: - section label - row of dots and connector lines - status line beneath
Dot states: - done = green - current = blue - next = neutral with dashed border
Style: - primary panel - subtle border - rounded large radius - highly compact
---
## Interaction Design
Interactions are restrained and fast.
### Hover - subtle opacity changes - mild background shift - small text-color emphasis - no large transforms - no dramatic animation
### Transition Timing - around `0.12s` to `0.15s` for simple hover state changes - fast and quiet
### Cursor - pointer only where clickable
### Emphasis Behavior The design should emphasize state through: - border color - chip background - local semantic tint - opacity changes - compact action controls
Avoid: - large motion - scale-heavy animation - shadow bloom - scroll theatrics
---
## Responsive Behavior
### Desktop - two-column layout - left workflow + narrow right rail - all sections visible at once
### Tablet - right rail can stack beneath the main workflow - keep panel order: 1. selected output 2. evaluator analysis 3. alternate outputs 4. next prompt 5. version tree 6. iteration panel
### Mobile - single-column stack - preserve compact app-shell feel - keep top bar controls visible - buttons may wrap - tree and iteration panels move below the main workflow
### Responsive Priorities - preserve section order - preserve semantic status colors - preserve compact hierarchy - do not turn this into a large editorial page
---
## Accessibility
### Contrast - maintain readable contrast across neutral surfaces - semantic status colors should remain legible for text and borders
### Focus - focus states should be visible on: - icon buttons - actions - override controls - textarea - tree nodes if interactive
### Legibility - keep tiny text readable through spacing and contrast - do not reduce opacity so much that alternate cards become illegible
### Interaction - clickable elements should have visible hover and focus behavior - semantic meaning should not rely on color alone; labels still matter
---
## Anti-Patterns
Do not reintroduce any of the following: - giant full-screen branch graph as the default layout - Swiss brutalist styling - thick black borders everywhere - all-uppercase oversized typography - heavy texture overlays - dark cinematic theme - glowing gradients - large animated backgrounds - dense dashboard metrics unrelated to the post-pick flow
Do not replace the current structure with: - compare-first card grid - landing page hero - giant timeline centerpiece - oversized sidebar navigation
This design system should stay tightly aligned to the actual HTML mockup: - compact top bar - left-column post-pick workflow - right-column compact tree and iterations - small semantic chips - winner-first hierarchy - understated product UI chrome
